titleStmt.title: The Warlock of the Glen: TEI edition

publicationStmt.distributor: Privately distributed by the Digital Lacy Project

availability.licence: The Lacy Project waives all rights to the TEI encoding applied to this material, which it believes to be in the public domain. You may copy, modify, distribute, or perform this work freely.

event.desc: Premiered at Theatre Royal, Covent Garden 2 Dec. 1820

revisionDesc.change: Add wikidata link to titleStmt where possible
Checked against UM copy; hand edited

titlePage.docImprint: London. Thomas Hailes Lacy, 89, Strand, W.C., (Opposite Southampton Street, Covent Garden Market)

performance.head: Warlock of the Glen
performance.p: First performed at the Theatre Royal Covent Garden, December 2, 1820.

castItem.roleDesc: the Rightful Heir, his Infant Nephew, aged 6 years
Rightful Laird of Glencairn, under the disguise of the Warlock of the Glen

castGroup.roleDesc: two Ruffians in the interest of Glencairn

castItem.roleDesc: Countess of Glencairn, and mother to Adelbert

front.p: Time Of Representation.—1 hour and 15 minutes.

list.item: Clanronald.—Scotch armour dress, scarf, fleshings, sandals, broadsword, &c.

The Warlock.—Ragged Scotch jacket, trunks, belt, fleshings, sandals, long black wig and beard, large cloak and a rugged branch. 2nd dress.—Handsome Scottish dress, fleshings, sandals, &c., boots, dark wig.
Adelbert. Scotch dress, fleshings, sandals and scarf.
Andrew. Fisherman's rough blue jacket, Scotch kilt, high boots, and plaid bonnet.
Murdock And Ruthven.—Breastplates, kilts, with scarfs and bonnets.

div.head: Scene First.—A Narrow Creek of the Sea.
div.stage: Fisherman's hut, R. 2 E.—set waters and ground pieces across—set return rock, R. 3 E., with platform—set return rock with raking platform on L. 3 E.
Boat appears from R. to C.—Andrew drawing up nets with Davie—paddles to shore.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Nae luck, nae luck, Davie, this cast; and sae we'll e'en gie ower, and haul up the auld cable to land. (he leaps out, hauls the boat to land, and assists Davie to hang up the nets) Past one o' the clock, and the lovers na' returned frae the kirk. Aweel, they'll be here anon; for ye ken we are to have a wee bit of junketting and merry-making upon the occasion. Aha, Davie! it does a body muckle gude to see the winsome bairns frolicking around ye, in aw the sunshine of youth and innocence. Ye ken, mon, our days are ower—lang syne, indeed! there was never a Highlander in aw the bonny set o' them would hae surpassed Andrew Mucklestane. Aweel, I maun gang to work a wee bit. Thae auld rotten deals, like their master, are somewhat warped wi' the wind and the sea, and maun be repaired o' necessity again the evening tide. There, ye may take the rest o' the tackle wi' ye—'twill dry well eneugh within.

div.stage: Exit Davie into the hut, R. 2 E.
Andrew takes a hammer and busies himself repairing the boat, at the same time humming a Scotch ballad)

sp.l: Auld Satan, Hornie, Nick, or Clootie,
Wha in your cavern, grim and sooty,
sp.stage: The Warlock enters down platform, L. 3 E. unperceived—his appearance bespeaks the most abject poverty; his garments, composed of tattered plaids, are carelessly thrown around him, his hair, long and thin, falls over his face, which, together with a matted beard and bushy eyebrows, renders his appearance most terrific—he advances towards Andrew, and leaning on a long staff, gazes attentively at him—Andrew, unconscious of being observed, continues his work and his song, till, accidentally turning round, he sees the Warlock standing, L. C. and starts back.
sp.p: Nay, sure!—yes, but it is. Why, Mattie, how the de'il— What does your coming hither portend?

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Woe to the house of Glencairn!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Mair's the pity. For, d'ye ken, what a heavy dispensation's hanging ower the house already?

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: I do. The Laird Clanronald usurps the birthright of his infant nephew, and, to secure it, wooes his brother's widow.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Ah, puir leddy! it's a sair change wi' her nooadays! she niver held up the head o' her, sin' her laird's departure for the wars, where he fell among the slain.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: He fell not in the wars, but by the assassin's steel on his return.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: True, it was sae whispered—but naebody kenned when or where; sure enough there weir nae witness.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: There was a witness—I myself beheld the deed!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: You—you a witness! O, my conscience, Mattie, I believe ye ha' to do wi' Satan! Indeed, folks do say, ye have mair dealings wi' him than mere words—else hoo do ye come by aw' that siller, which o' times ye are sae lavish wi'? Aweel it's nae affair o' mine; ainly ye'll do me the justice to say I niver received ony o' it; for ye ken I minded the auld proverb, which saith, "What's got ower the deevil's back, is spent unde his belly." (a distant strain of music is heard, L. U. E. with marriage bells, &c. the Warlock starts, and is going, R.) How noo, Mattie—whither awa'? It's nae but the chiels returning fra' the wedding.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p:
Then must I not be seen. My business is with you alone. Come hither! (beckoning him authoritatively) You must
promise—nay, swear to meet me to-night upon the moor, by the ruins of the abbey, as the castle clock shall toll the hour of twelve.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Meet you on the muir! Mercy o' me, I'd as soon think of meeting auld Nick! for ye ken that said twelve o' the clock, inasmuch as it is midnight, is an awfu' time to meet sic a gaberlunzie as the likes o' you; and particularly when a third, and far less agreeable companion than yoursel, Mattie—I speak in respect of Satan—might pop upon us, and claim ac- quaintanceship.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Then mark my words, for surely as I speak, shall it befall you. Ne'er shall you know an hour of peace your boats shall be wrecked—your house, and all that it contains, burned to the ground; yourself reduced to beggary, wander an outcast from your native land, and call on death in vain to ease your pangs, if you obey not my injunctions. Mark me I've said it, and I've sworn it—I, Matthew of the Glen, that ne'er was know to break my word!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Haud your clavers, for the love o' gudeness, Mattie you terrify me. Yet what for should I fear? the de'il can't ha' any reasonable claim upon me; and as to his imps, and the likes o' them, I'm sure sma' pleasure can it gie to skelp and worry a puir man like me; therefore I'll meet you.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Swear!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: (proudly)I've said it—ye hae my word—a Highlander's word—be satisfied.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Enough; I'll trust you! (Music—He waves his staff, disappears, R. 3 [A-Z][A-Z]+E.)

div.stage: Enter Highland Lads and Lasses, decorated with favours, over platform, L. 3 E., dancing, preceding Sandie and Marian.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Well, here we are, Master Mucklestane, fast as the kirk can make us; as happy and handsome a couple as ever plighted faith.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Ah, my bonnie bairn, gie ye joy! (shaking his hand) By your leave, bonny lassie. (saluting her) Welcome, chiels, welcome. But ye dinna ken what an unco' strange visitor I hae had; nae less a personage than "Mat o' the Glen!"

sp.speaker: All
sp.p: "Mat o' the Glen!"

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Has that awful gaberlunzie appeared again? for certain, his coming bodes no good.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p:
O' my conscience, ye may weel say sae. But ye'll be the mair surprised when I tell ye he appointed me to be—
The Warlock, wrapped in a large cloak, who has entered unobserved, advances behind him and whispers "Silent!" then disappears, L. 2 E. unseen by the rest. When he appointed me to be "silent." (terrified) It wa' himself. Wha but him? wha but him?

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: But who, Andrew?

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: The the—de'il, I believe; and noo it's out. (all laugh)

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Why, the mon looks for all the world as though he had seen a bogle.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Whisht! hae a care o' yoursel'—he's within hearing.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: He! whom do you mean?

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: (recovering himself) Oh, naebody, naebody! I was but communing wi' mysel'. But come, ye dinna forget the dance, sirs! The music, there the music! strike up, Donald, and foot it lightly.

div.stage: Music. A lively Scotch reel. At the conclusion the Countess Adela screams without, L. 3 E., and enters, bearing her Child in her arms, descends the rock, L. 3 E., and rushes into the midst—the music and dance pause abruptly.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: (C.)Oh, save me! protect my child!

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: (L. C.)'Tis the Countess of Glencain. I know her well—she, who, they say, through grief for her lost lord, became distracted.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Distracted! oh, credit not the vile reports of sinful men, invented for the worst of purposes! attribute not a mother's terrors to the wild ravings of a disordered mind. I am not mad. This is my child I hold my child that they wonld tear from me, and murder. Oh, Heaven, I am too sensible of my unhappy state!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Unfortunate leddy! what can we do for ye?—auld Andrew speaks to ye, be wha has ken'd ye in fair weather and in foul; and wha niver will desert ye while this gude arm can wield a claymore.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Men of Glencairn, I call upon you as a mistress—as a mother! Behold this boy, your late revered master's son—the rightful heir of Glencairn. I call upon you to protect him! Oh, save him from his unnatural uncle, who, not content with usurpation, would stamp his claim to these dominions in blood—the heart's blood of this poor child.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p:
Ah, my dear leddy, an we had as gude power as we hae gude will and gude cause! but ye ken the laird's
unco potent, and I fear me, might claim a right o' guardianship ower the bairn; and, in that case—

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: A right of guardianship! he, the usurper! sooner would I plunge the boy into the roaring tide—sooner consign him to the mercy of the waves than to his savage uncle's power. Andrew, you once were foremost in zeal to the house of Glencairn; then— Oh, if you still have the heart—the courage to assist your master's wife, exert it now. My father's castle stands but three leagues hence; could I reach, the doors would open to protect me. What need of words? your fishing boat would be a secret and secure conveyance. Will you—Oh, will you aid a fallen wretch like me, whose means accord not with her wishes to reward you?

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Reward? what for an act o' duty? Leddy, I hae but little phrase wi' me; but this I maun say, that he wha will na lend a helping hand to a leddy in distress may wear the form o' a mon, but maun hae the heart o' a demon.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Generous man, accept a mother's thanks.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Come, neebors, disperse yoursel's, each to his ain hame, and look ye keep close counsel about what ye hae heard, for, ye ken, it's ill o' the vassal's mooth that betrays the bread it eats.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Never fear; we know our duty, and will abide by it. Come along, Marian. (Music. Exeunt Sandie, Marian, and Peasants, R. 3 E.)

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Come, my pretty bairn, and only let me see the stoutest he will hurt a hair belonging of ye, while auld Andrew can wag a finger in your defence.

div.stage: Re-enter Sandie, R. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Andrew! Maister Andrew—

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: How now? What the de'il ails ye? Canna ye speak, mon?

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: There are two of the laird's black-visaged gentry coming along the beach; I ken them from the rocks. Should they be in pursuit of the countess—

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: (snatching her child) Oh, yes! I know them well—the agents of the fell Clanronald's power! Whither can I fly?

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: I hae it; betake yoursel' to the wood, and make your way as secretly as ye can to the eastern cliff; in an hoor hence my boat shall be in readiness. Sandie will be your guide—he kens the way fu' weel.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p:
Ay, that I do, uncle; and a precious abode of unclean spirits it is. But never fear; I'll bring you safely
through, in spite of all the hobgoblins, bogles, and worriecoows in Christendom.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: That's a bra'e lad. Ha! they are here! Make haste, my bonny leddy, for the love o' gudeness; I will manage to detain them.

div.stage: Music. The Countess, attended by Sandie, hurries out with the Child, R. 1 E. while Andrew appears as if busily engaged with his nets, R. 2 E.
Enter Ruthven and Murdock, down platform, L. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: I am certain she must have passed this way.

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: Let us question the fisherman; he may know something.

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: Hollo, old fellow! (going up and slapping him on the back)

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Fellow! familiar eneugh at first sight, methinks. What want ye wi' Andrew Mucklestane? (coming forward)

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: (C.)We want a lady that—

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Ye want a leddy; aweel, mon, I canna satisfy ye.

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: (L.)An unfortunate maniac, that contrived this morning to elude the vigilance of her keepers.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Hoot awa'! that wa' niver a fault o' mine. What for do ye tax me, mon? Ye dinna tak' it into your stupid heeds that I ha' run off wi' a lunatic?

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: No, no, friend; but we suspect that you have given her concealment. Answer; have you not seen such a person as we describe pass this way? Answer, or tremble at the consequence. Know you not whence we come?

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Ay, as weel as I do know where ye'll gang ain day or ither. But be ye laird or loon, gentle or simple, I'll just tell ye I'm na minded to let my tongue bring me into trouble.

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: Indeed! then we'll make so bold as to search your cottage. (they are proceeding to the hut, R. when Andrew, seizing an oar, stations himself before the door.)

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Hauld, aback! or, by St. Andrew, my respectable namesake, I'll mak sae bold, as to dismiss ye, and wi' shirts fu' o' sair banes into the bargain.

div.stage: Enter Clanronald and Two Vassals, down platform, L. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: How now! my officers threatened! Slave, retire, nor dare to oppose my servants in the performance of their duty.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p:
The laird! then I maun yield to necessity—I ken
my distance, and never shall it be said o' Andrew Mucklestane he forgot his duty to the hoose o' Glencairn. He retires; Clanronald waves his hand to Ruthven and Murdock, who enter the hut, R. 2 E. followed by Andrew.

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Confusion! to be thus baffled by a woman! frustrated in all my deep-laid schemes of aggrandisement! What boots it to have crushed the serpent, if the young adder still survive to sting my future peace? Yet wherefore should I rack my soul with fears? They cannot long escape me; and once more in my power, some means shall soon be found to make the lady mine. What, though she scorns my offers—force shall effect my purpose. Re-enter Ruthven and Murdock from hut, R. 2 E. No welcome tidings yet? (they shahe their heads) 'Tis very strange. Perchance she may have sought the forest, as promising the best concealment. Follow me instantly; I swear never to know a moment's rest till these accursed fugitives be found. Exit, followed by Vassals, R.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Ah, gang thy ways—auld Beelzebub will claw ye at last. Puir leddy! I maun na forget ye! na, na; let aw the warld desert ye, auld Andrew will stand by ye, sink or swim. (Music. Leaps into boat and pushes from shore to R.)

div.head: Scene Second. A Romantic Glen (2nd Grooves.) amid the rocks.
div.stage: A cavern, L. C., concealed by overhanging brambles, practicable—bank on R. Lights half down.
Enter Donald and Allen, L. 1 E. bearing a basket of provisions, flagon, &c.

sp.speaker: Allen
sp.p: See, this is the spot, and there the cave where he is supposed to dwell—so place the provisions at the entrance, and away. I would not ha' him find us loitering here for a' the gude things we ha' brought to appease him. (puts basket down at mouth of the cave, L. C.)

sp.speaker: Donald
sp.p: Tut, man! he has nae power to harm honest folk like us! Here has he dwelt six lang years, and never injured mortal man that I ever heard of. (aside) 'Tis as weel to speak respectfully of the auld deeirt, in case he should overhear.

sp.speaker: Allen
sp.p: Why, you won't pretend to deny he has dealings with evil spirits, brings blight upon the corn, and murrain among the cattle—nay, and I'll warrant me, had a hand in the death of our good laird, wha disappeared in sic a strange manner, just aboot the time this Mattie was first seen.

sp.speaker: Donald
sp.p:
Pooh, nonsense! dinna believe a word of it. For
my part, I can't see what folks have to fear in him. I dare swear he is a civil-spoken sort of a body after all.

sp.speaker: Allen
sp.p: Hark! I hear footsteps—he's coming! Run—run, fellow!

sp.speaker: Donald
sp.p: (frightened) Coming! you don't say so!

sp.speaker: Allen
sp.p: Why, you seem alarmed! He has nae power over honest folks, you know.

sp.speaker: Donald
sp.p: Ay, very true! but we are none of us perfect, you ken. At any rate, I'm off. Music—Exeunt, L. 1 E.

div.stage: Enter Sandie and the Countess, with her Child, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Nay, bear up, madam, we must be near the cliff.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: No more—I can no more—my wearied feet refuse their office, and exhausted nature sinks within me.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Then sit awhile upon this bank, and recruit your strength.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Rest here, my precious burden. (Countess and Child sit on bank, R.) O, while you remain to me I am not wholly wretched.

sp.speaker: Adel.
sp.p: Dear mother, I'm sadly hungry.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Say you so? (looking round) Eh? as I live, a basket of provisions and a flagon of wine. Egad, this is rare luck!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Mysterious Providence! Some unknown supplies our wants. (taking the basket from Sandie)

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: O, ay—some unknown! Madam, madam! do not eat for your life! This is the Warlock's cave we had better be gone!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: In sooth, not yet. It could be no unkind spirit has sent us these. There, my child. (giving food)

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Oh, madam, you don't know half their tricks! What if it should be poisoned? Why, odds life, you won't eat! Eh? Now if I dared—there's nobody by—so here goes. (sits down on stage, near bank, and eats) I'faith, it's excellent. Here, my little dear, take a sup of this, it will warm your little heart. What, you won't? Then I will. (drinks) Egad, your spirits live daintily. Whiskey, as I'm a sinner.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Where are your fears now, Sandie?

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: (drinking) Going, going, gone! (turns the flagon topsy-turvy) quite gone!. By my valour, I feel as bold as a lion. Let 'em come on a whole legion of spirits, damme if I budge.

sp.speaker: Adel.
sp.p: How coldly the wind blows around us!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Say'st thou, my child? Then, perchance, this cavern will afford us shelter. (they cross towards the cave, L. C.)

sp.speaker: Warlock
sp.p: (without, L. C.) Back, back!

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: (starting up) Heaven help us! what voice was that?

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: It issued from the cavern.

sp.speaker: Warlock
sp.p: (within, L. C.) Who dares disturb the solitude of the Warlock?

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: The Warlock! (trembling) Oh dear, oh dear! then it is all up with us!

div.stage: Music—The Warlock darts from the cave, L. C. and starts at beholding the Countess.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Ha, a woman!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: (R. C.) Yes, and a mother. As thou art human, let that word disarm thee of the power to harm us.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: (crosses to Sandie) And who art thou?

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: (R. falling on his knees) I'm neither a father or a mother yet—I'm only a miserable wretch, not worth your notice, for I am just married — Oh, spare me spare me, for I'm punished enough for one day !

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Fly! hence! begone!

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: With all the pleasure in life! He runs out, R. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: What may this mean? Nay, do not tremble so, my child.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Thy child! thine! Ha, let me look upon him. (he crosses, and grasps the Child with fervour)

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: (R.) Oh, Heaven! what would you do?

sp.speaker: Adel.
sp.p: (L.) Do not fear, mother! I'm sure he will not harm me!

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Harm you! not for worlds! or if I did, it could be but with kindness. (passing him across to R. C.) There, take him—tear him from me, while yet these arms can loose their hold, or my heart bursts not in the struggle.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Alas, unhappy man! his wits are crazed!

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Not so! I know thee well—thee and thy sorrows. Adela of Glencairn, dangers beset you, guilt is busy to confound you—but fear not, for I will protect you. In the hour of peril, when all other earthly means have failed, call upon me, I will be near to help thee—I, Matthew of the Glen, who never was known to break my word.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Thanks, thanks! Come, my child, let us proceed. Oh, surely Heaven will look down with pity, and save this innocent from the fell grasp of his pursuers! Generous man, adieu! Music—Exit with the Child, L. 1 E.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: (gazing intently after them) They take the way towards the cliff. Now I may follow unseen and unsuspected. Oh, nature, yet– yet a little longer! bear me up to raise the fallen and confound the guilty. Music—Exit, L. 1 E.

div.head: Scene Third.— View of the Sea and Rocky Shore.
div.stage: A huge cliff, with platform, which projects over the water, R. 4 E.— rock on L.—set waters and ground pieces. Lights half down.
Music—Enter the Countess, leading her Child, L. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: This is the place! Cheer up, my lovely boy, our toils are passed. There stands the cliff, from whose dizzy height so oft, in happy years, I have gazed upon the fearful deep. (looks towards the sea) It must be past the time Andrew appointed—he said an hour! surely he meant not to deceive us! Ah, no, I know him well! the good old man could never play a faithless part—he will be here anon.

sp.speaker: Ruthven
sp.p: (without, L. U. E. distant) Hollo !

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: What noise was that? (she listens fearfully) Oh, 'twas but the wave dashing against the shore. A mother's terrors imagine danger in every passing breeze.

sp.speaker: Ruthven
sp.p: (without, L. U. E.—louder) Hollo!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Gracious Heaven! surely that was more than fancy! I heard voices!

sp.speaker: Clanronald
sp.p: (without, L.) To the cliff! Search among the rocks!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Oh heavens! my pursuers! (Music—She catches up the Child, and, staggering to rock, R. falls exhausted against it)

div.stage: Enter Clanronald, Ruthven, and Murdock, L. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Ha, madam, have I discovered you? Now tear the child from her embrace.

div.stage: She screams, and clings to the Boy—they advance to take it, when, at the moment, the Warlock appears on the cliff, R. and waves them back.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Forbear! (Tableau)

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: (L.) Who dares to dictate to Clanronald?

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: I dare! I, Matthew of the Glen!

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Insolent! think you the superstitious tales of peasants have any influence on me? Mummery all! Retire, or dread my anger!

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Ha, dost thou menace, and dare to doubt the power that I possess to awe the guilty? Then mark, and let thy stubborn soul shrink in the dread conviction of my truth, while I proclaim thee "Fratricide!" Is not thy nature satiate with blood? (thunder) Beware! the wrath of Heaven hangs over thee! tempt not its vengeance further. He disappears—Clanronald remains petrified for a time, till, recovering his self-command, he exclaims in a tone of determined authority)

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Why look ye idly on– has the weird madman's words made statues of you? Seize the child, I say!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Stand off approach me not! my griefs have made me desperate! You dare not tear him from me! Dare not? Oh, no! you will not—you could not do it! If 'tis his wealth you covet, take it all– enjoy it, if you can, so you but spare his life! The scene I have just witnessed shall never be revealed—I and my child will fly to some distant land, and never return to trouble you.

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: How passing fair is supplicating woman!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: (starting indignantly from the ground) Ha, do you mock my sufferings? Man, I have borne much—Heaven truly knows how much! But pause ere you inflict a wound beyond a mother's power to endure! (with dignity) Dare to divide me from my child, and I denounce you to the world—a murderer!

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: These are the frantic exclamations of a maniac! (seizing the Child, and throwing him to L.) There, fellows, take the boy—away with him to the castle.

sp.speaker: Adel.
sp.p: O mother – mother! save me from these wicked men! Exit Murdock with the Child, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: I come, my child! I follow you! (endeavours to pass Clanronald, who seizes her– the Countess recovering, endeavours feebly to escape)

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Nay, lady, your efforts are in vain.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Off, monster! murderer! Let me pass! (looking round, she misses the Boy, and gives a loud scream) My child! Gone—for ever gone! Oh, horrible! My husband, and my infant, too—both dead! murdered by thy blood-stained hand! But there's a way left yet! Thus I escape from you! Music—She breaks from his hold, and rushes out, R. 2 E.—she reappears upon the cliff, R. 4 E. and plunges into the water; at this moment Andrew is seen in his fishing boat, making towards the cliff, from L. U. E.

div.stage: Warlock enters from R. 4 E.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Now, Andrew—the boat! the boat!

div.stage: Tableau. Boat, with Andrew and Countess. Warlock on cliff. Clan. and Vassals.

div.head: Scene First.—
div.stage: Interior of a Cottage. (3rd Grooves.) Table set for supper on L. C.—lamp on table—one rustic chair on R. C. —three stools on L.—door L. C. practicable, backed by dark wood. Thunder, lightning, and rain.
Sandie, Marian, and Mause discovered at supper table on L.

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Mercy on us, what a night is this! it blows, rains, thunders, and lightens enough to blow in the casement. Heaven help the poor countess !

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Ah, I'll warrant me you may spare your prayers, good mother! by this time she's past all earthly help. Why, to judge by his looks, "Mat o' the Glen" wouldn't make above one meal of her!

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: The voracious cormorant!

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: Why, you don't think he devoured her, Sandie?

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Ah, that did he, body and breeches, I'll be bound for him. It wasn't for nothing he sent me out of the way.

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Depend upon it, children, this is an awful season! Have I not heard creaking noises all night? and the owl screech, and the raven crook, and sic like unnatural bodements? My life on't, the devil is going his rounds. Heaven shield us from his unwholesome clutch!

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: But go on, good mother—you were telling us of the poor Laird of Glencairn.

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Very true. Ay, it's just now six years agone, last Candlemas, that he took leave of his lady, and left his home for the wars, whence, poor man, he was never fated to return.

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: And what is it supposed became of him?

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Oh, you may well ask! but nobody can answer that. It was given out, indeed, he fell in the field of battle, while some go so far as to say he was privately set upon and murdered.

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: Murdered!

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: And was it not suspected that—

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Whist, my bairn, speak lower! for walls have ears now-a-days. True, it was suspected that the present laird— (a loud clap of thunder) Mercy on us! what a hurly-burly was there!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: (without, R. c.) Hollo !

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Hush! Didn't I hear a voice? (the cottage door is shaken) Again! (All frightened) What can it be? (a violent thumping at the door, accompanied with thunder and lightning)

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Oh, it's his ghost—it's his ghost! Oh, I shall faint, I know I shall!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: (without) Open the door!

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Ah, you may hollo till you are hoarse—you'll find no entrance here, unless it's through the keyhole.

div.stage: The door is burst open with a loud crash, and Andrew enters, R. C. bearing the Countess—all scream.

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: Why, 'tis my uncle—'tis Andrew!

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: (recovering) Good faith, so it is! a woman too!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Ay, a woman. It's nae the first I hae had in my arms, that let me tell ye. Gie us a chair, wull ye? (they give him a chair—he places the Countess upon it)

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: Heavens! the countess! and dripping wet!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Aweel, lassie, ye'll nae be fashed o' that neither, when I tell ye she's but just come out o' the sea.

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Pray tell us—I long to hear how it all came about.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Dinna ye ask, woman—dinna ye ask; but I fear she was flying frae cruel, wicked men.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Oh, Mattie, Mattie! this is your handy work!

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: Then where is the child?

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: The child! (he starts, and appears violently agitated) Did you say the child? (with a look of frenzy, he clasps his hands together, exclaiming) Gone! lost and gone! I hae left him struggling in the waves! (he throws himself on a stool, leans his head on the table, and appears overcome with grief—they go up to him)

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: (crosses to him) Andrew; dear uncle, be pacified.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: (repelling them) Be quiet, will ye? be quiet. I tell ye I hae murdered him! I loved him better than my ain heart's bluid, and I hae murdered him!

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: Do not take on so do not think of it!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Nae think o' it? but I maun think o' it. What can I tell his mither when she comes to hersel', and asks for her bairn? Hoo—hoo can I answer her? Oh, this auld heart, that hae weathered out sae mony a gale, maun find this tug too much! Yet what for do I stand clavering here? I'll seek for him, gin he war fifty fathom deep; I'll pluck him frae the waves, and—

sp.speaker: Warlock
sp.p: (without, exclaims) Andrew Mucklestane! the clock has struck! (All start)

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: St. Bridget defend us! That was no earthly voice!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: It is –it's Mattie! Ah, he'll gie me speerings o' the bairn! Coming, Mattie, coming. He rushes out, D. R. C.

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: Follow him, for Heaven's sake, Sandie! He knows not what he does.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Follow him?—fcllow a madman? odds flesh! that's sooner said than done. I'll see the road he takes, however, if that will please you. Exit door, R. с.

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Come ye hither, child, the lady's coming to herself. (the Countess opens her eyes, but fixes them on vacancy)

sp.speaker: Marian
sp.p: How terrible she looks! Lady, dear lady, do you not know us?

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Know you? Yes, you are the murderers of my child! But he is in heaven now, where you can never reach him.

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Poor insensible thing—her wits are clean gone!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: See, he stretches out his little arms to me he smiles on me his smile was ever beautiful! I come to you, my child! Why do you hold me down? Tis cruel to keep me from my babe.

div.stage: Sandie rushes in from door, R. C.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Here's a business!

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: Why, what's the matter, Sandie?

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: The laird! the laird! He's coming with a troop at his heels! the countess will be carried from us.

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: We shall be all murdered! Come this way, my lady! (endeavouring to lead her off, R., she resists.)

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: No, I will meet him.

div.stage: Enter Clanronald, Ruthven, Murdock, and Two Vassals from D. R. C., bearing torches.

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: (L. C.) Behold the object of our search in a peasant's cottage! Quick! secure her!

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Ha, fratricide! Dost thou dare to tear him from me? Where is my child? my child, thou infant murderer? (her strength fails, and she falls into Ruthven'S arms, R.)

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Convey her to the castle instantly, and prepare the nuptial rites—this very hour shall our marriage be consummated.

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: My laird, allow me to observe that taking a leddy agin her will is not exactly—

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Utter another word, and you shall become for life the tenant of my castle's deepest dungeon—begone!

sp.speaker: Sandie
sp.p: Poor dear lady! my heart aches for her, doomed to the arms of such a bear.

sp.speaker: Mause
sp.p: A bear—a Hottentot! ugh!

div.stage: Exeunt Sandie, Mause, and Marian, reluctantly, L.

sp.p: (aside) Murdock, to you I give the charge to see the brat safely disposed of. You may throw him beside the ruins of the abbey; there let him moulder beside his father's bones. Away! Murdock bows assent, and Clanronald with his train, bearing the Countess, exeunt through D. R. C.

div.head: Scene Second. —
div.stage: The Moor, by midnight. (1st and 2nd grooves) On R. the ruins of an abbey, surrounded by a few withered trees; the wind is heard at intervals, and the thunder as dying away in the distance; lights down.
Enter Andrew, L. 2 E. fearfully looking round him.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Mattie! Mattie! what for dinna ye answer me, mon? Is it creditable, think ye, to invite folk to meet ye in your ain drawing room, as it were, and after aw, na appear to bid 'em welcome? (the bell, shaken by the wind, tolls) Wha gangs there? speak! Ye had better, (drawing his hanger) or, by the word of a Highlander, ye sall niver wag the saucy tongue o' ye again. What a goustie place is this—ony company war preferable to this dreadful stillness. Why, Mattie, I say—canny Mattie! bonny Mattie! where i' the name of all the bogles and worriecows hae ye betaken yoursel?

div.stage: The Warlock steals on, L. 2 E. unobserved by Andrew.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Peace! I charge you.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Sae ye are come at last. But what gars ye glow'r a hint ye o' that gate? Ye hae na' brought wi' ye ony companion, it is to be hoped worse than yoursel?

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Murder is abroad!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: (trembling) Mur-murder! ye dinna say sae! For the love o' gudeness, dinna mak' jeer o' me. Ye terrify me, Mattie!

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: They come this way—the demons! Retire; conceal yourself among the ruins.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Demons coming this way? My certes, Mattie, tak' me wi' ye—ye're preferable to a demon, ony day o' the week. They retire behind the ruins, R. 2 E.

div.stage: Enter Ruthven and Murdock, L. 2 E. bearing the Child, wrapped in a large cloak..

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: This is the place! beneath those stones rots the father's body. Come, Ruthven, despatch. (they remove the cloak, and set down the Child)

sp.speaker: Adel.
sp.p: Where are you taking me? all is so dark around.

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: Are you frightened, boy?

sp.speaker: Adel.
sp.p: I should be, were I not going to my mother. Pray let us make haste –this is such a frightful place.

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: Pshaw! we trifle time. Come, Ruthven, draw your poignard while I go scoop a grave among the rubbish.

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: By Heaven! he clings to me in confidence. I cannot harm him.

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: What, have you scruples? pshaw! away with them. What beholds us?

sp.speaker: Warlock
sp.p: (from the abbey) The eye of Heaven!

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: Some one spoke. Would I were quit of this.

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: Fancy, all.

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: It might have been; but, as I'm a living man, it seemed the warning of Glencairn!

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: Coward! art thou afraid of ghosts?

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: I know not how it is, but my nature revolts from killing a child.

sp.speaker: Adel.
sp.p: You certainly do not mean to kill me! and yet you look so frowningly. Pray do not hurt me! (kneeling)

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: In faith, I cannot.

sp.speaker: Murd.
sp.p: Away, then, thou slave to conscience! leave me to do the job; but expect not to share in the reward.

sp.speaker: Ruth.
sp.p: Leave you to the full reward? never! This to prevent it! (he is preparing to plunge the poignard into him, when Andrew rushes out, exclaiming.)

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: It is my leddy's bairn! Have at ye baith, ye precious pair of villains! Music. He attacks them furiously, and drives them off, L. 2 E.

div.stage: At this moment the Warlock darts from the ruins, snatches up the Child, and exclaiming, "Mine! mine!" rushes out, R. 1 E. Re-enter Andrew, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Ye hae gotten it noo, or I hae mistaken my weapon—a decent wame fu' o' steel And now for the bairn; gude faith, how pleased his mither wul be! Come, my little dear! (turns round) Ha, gane! then Mattie has gotten his claws on him. Mattie, for the love of gudeness, mon, dinna harm the child. Mattie! Mattie! Mattie! (Music.—Exit, R. 2 E. he is heard calling without, then re-enters, R. 1 E.) It's aw vain! the de'il a blink hae I had o' him; he is gane—gane for ever! sae I'll niver waste my een with greeting; I'll bear it as an auld seamen ooght. (wiping his eyes) Pshaw! this hulk is rotten, and the salt water oozes through in spite o' me. Would I could see this Mattie once again—but once! and if ever I took these bull dogs frae his ill-boding throat till I had shaken aw the breath out o' his ault farrant carcass, say I'm nae Highlander.

div.stage: Enter the Warlock, R. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: (striking his staff on the ground) What would you with the Warlock of the Glen?.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Ha, you here! tell me, where is the boy? Nae, niver bend your scornfu' eyes o' me; I'd speak my mind, gin you were twice as frightfu', and that's na' in nature. Speak! tell me what hae ye done wi' the bairn, or I'll wring it frae your saul, ye unearthly mortal.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Be satisfied—the boy is safe.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: But does he live? only tell me that he lives.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: He still lives.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Bless ye—bless ye! (he is overcome by joy, and falls at the Warlock's feet) Mattie, ye have brought me to my knees. I niver thought to hae bent sae low to mortal man, much less to—but I hae done! Mattie, ye hae ta'en a load o' anguish off frae my heart, and mony thanks to ye for the favour.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: (aside) I can resist no longer! (then with tone of his voice and manner altered) Rise, Andrew, rise, and hear me! Whom dost thou think I am? I know thou say'st, "The Warlock! a weird recluse!" but look upon me. (removing his hair, &c.) Andrew! has thou forgotten Glencairn?

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Alive! na, sure! Yes, but it is! my een dinna deceive me it is the laird himsel'! (kneels)

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: My poor, faithful servant!

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: But he's alive! I hae him! I touch him! Oh, my auld heart will burst! But the Countess—the bairn! the—ha, ha, ha!—you, Mattie! you the laird!

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Yes, Andrew, for these six long years, beneath the garb of misery, have I dragged out a wretched being. Such were the terms imposed on me by the repentant who preserved my life.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Your life?

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: Even so. When the fell poignard of a villain brother struck me to the earth, his myrmidon, commissioned to entomb my body, discovered the vital spark was not extinct within me, and with a soul less hardened than his master's, removed me to a cave, where, day by day, I gained fresh strength; and he there extorted, as the price of life, an oath that I would never to mortal man reveal myself, till he should be no more; that oath I have kept inviolate, till yesterday, stung by remorse for all his numerous sins, the wretch expired and freed me from restraint.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: Oh, happy day! then ye'll return amang us?

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: I shall return to claim again my long-lost rights; but we must proceed with caution. Will you assist me?

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: With my life.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: The knowledge of a secret entrance to the castle will aid my purpose well; thither must we proceed without delay; for I have formed a scheme, which, if I err not, shall confound the guilty, even in the fulness of their triumph. Come, come! Exit L. 1 E.

div.head: Scene Third.—
div.stage: Interior of a Gothic Chapel, brilliantly illuminated. (3rd and 5th grooves) Large practicable door, C. backed with altar lighted, and steps.
Music.—Clanronald, Ruthven, and Servants discovered.

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Ay, this looks well; these nuptial torches blaze with becoming splendour. Quick; lead the countess hither. Exeunt Ruthven and Murdock, R. 1 E. Now, now, Clanronald, thou hast arrived at fortune's pinnacle. But by what means? no matter! let dotards shrink at the qualms of conscience; I spurn at its dominion! (a strain of music is heard, R) Ha, that peal proclaims my bride's approach. She comes to crown my dearest wishes. (Music, forte) Enter the Countess, R. 1., with Ruthven and Murdock, attended by Ladies. This is well, madam! I am joyed to find that reason has assumed it sway, and you no longer shrink from the calls of love.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Deceive not thyself, Clanronald. I swear before these witnesses, who will record my words, never to pronounce vows that bind me to a murderer.

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: I'll hear no more! force shall effect –this way, to the altar. (dragging her to the C. doors, she struggles)

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: Just Heaven! is there no help? no hope? Ha! (with a sudden burst of recollection) I call upon the Warlock of the Glen!

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Indeed! Let him appear to save thee, if he can. (he has, by this time, reached the folding doors, when they open suddenly; the altar is discovered illuminated, before which stands the Warlock; Clanronald looses his hold of the Countess, and staggers back, L.—all express amazement—tableau) Wherefore art thou here?

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: To tell thee thou hast filled up the measure of thy guilt. Thy murdered victims cry for justice on thee. Villain!

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: I will not hear thee. Ho, there! seize that vile impostor!

div.stage: Murdock and Ruthven, R. advance to seize him—the Warlock waves them back.

sp.speaker: Warl.
sp.p: (to Clanronald, L.) Obdurate! dost thou still doubt my power? then will I raise a witness thou canst not, dar'st not question. Behold! (throwing off the Warlock habit, and appearing as Glencairn) Thy brother!

div.stage: Clanronald is thunderstruck—the Countess, with a scream of joy, rushes into the arms of her husband. Vassals enter R. and L.

sp.speaker: Glen.
sp.p: My wife! my Adela!

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Ha! do the dead rise against me? Could not the grave withhold its prey? Why—why is this? I struck him to the earth! Thou shalt not triumph! this dagger failed me once, but now — (attempts to stab Glencairn, but is seized by the Countess, and disarmed by Two Vassals, L.)

sp.speaker: Glen.
sp.p: Hold thy desperate hand! and rather, by repentance, seek atonement and forgiveness for thy crimes.

sp.speaker: Clan.
sp.p: Never will I repent! and even in my fall, I triumph, for your only child, the infant is dead—he has perished by my orders!

div.stage: Enter Andrew with the Child, L. 3 E.

sp.speaker: Andrew
sp.p: It's a lie! it's a lie! The bairn is here! safe and sound! I hae gotten him, and I'll gie him to his mother.

sp.speaker: Countess
sp.p: My husband and my child, too, alive—both alive! Just heaven, I thank thee! (Music. She raises her hands—Glencairn embraces her— Andrew kneels with the Child, L. C.)

div.stage: Ladies Countess Adel. Glen. Andrew. Clan. Vassals.
Curtain

list.head: Stage Directions
list.item: R. means Right of the Stage facing the Audience;
D.F. Door in the Flat; or Scene running across the back of the Stage;
L.C.F. Left Door in the Flatl
